title: Insider Trading Restrictions and Informed Trading in Peer Stocks
abstract: Using a uniquely constructed data set of trades by corporate insiders in all stocks, we find that, after insider trading regulations become stricter, insiders are 20% more likely to trade in peer stocks and that such trades become more profitable. The increase in both the probability and profitability of peer-stock trades is driven by the insider’s information that is fungible to industry peers. Stricter insider trading laws are designed to improve liquidity and price informativeness in capital markets. We show that peer trading dampens these intended benefits of the insider trading regulation.

author.Prachi Deuskar: Finance Area, Indian School of Business, Hyderabad 500111, India
author.Aditi Khatri: Cornerstone Research, New York, New York 10022
author.Jayanthi Sunder: Dhaliwal-Reidy School of Accountancy, University of Arizona, Tucson, Arizona 85721

article.1. Introduction: Insider trading laws are often justified using arguments of fairness and integrity of financial markets. Stricter insider trading laws can reduce adverse selection and improve liquidity in capital markets by limiting managers’ ability to profit from their superior firm-specific information. Yet if insider trading laws are too strict, they may hamper the functioning of financial markets by making compliance too burdensome. Thus, any regulator has to perform a balancing act while designing an insider trading regime. We study this trade-off by understanding the implications of a new insider trading law in India, which strengthened the rules prohibiting insiders’ trades in their own firms but not in other firms. We examine whether managers, at least partially, substitute insider trades with informed trades in peer-firm stocks based on fungible information. We find that they do. We then investigate the implications of such substitution for liquidity and stock price informativeness, two market attributes tied to insider trading laws’ objectives. A significant challenge in addressing these questions is the lack of data on insiders’ trades in peer-firm stocks because these trades are not disclosed. Further, a clean identification of the effect of insider trading laws requires significant changes in the regulation. To overcome the first challenge, we use a proprietary data set to capture insiders’ trades in their own firms and peer firms. We match the reported trades of Indian corporate insiders with a proprietary data set of all trades on the BSE (formerly the Bombay Stock Exchange), a major stock exchange in India. The BSE trade data set has anonymized trader identifiers that allow us to track the same trader throughout our sample, enabling us to examine trades by matched insiders even in stocks where they are not insiders for the period 2012 through 2017. To cleanly identify a shock to insider trading regulation, we exploit the 2015 Prohibition of Insider Trading Regulation by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI). The Indian setting allows us to understand the implications of a significant new regulation, something that is unlikely in developed markets, given their more mature insider trading regulation and case laws. The new regulation was adopted to overhaul insider trading laws and increase the enforcement powers of SEBI, and many of the changes were shaped by insider trading laws in the United States and the United Kingdom (Dey 2016). The financial news media described the regulation as stricter than the older regulation and in line with global standards (Economic Times 2015,Prasad and Khan 2015). One crucial change in the new law was that it put the burden of proof on insiders to show that they were not in possession of unpublished price-sensitive information (UPSI) while trading in their own firms’ stock. However, in all other cases (which include trading in peer stocks), the burden of proof remained on the SEBI. This distinction sought to achieve stricter enforcement of insider trading without excessively curtailing all informed trading.1 In the context of the new regulation, we examine (i) the changes in trading behavior and profitability of trades by insiders in their own stocks and peer stocks, and (ii) the consequences of this change for liquidity and price informativeness in the market. Absent legal ramifications of insider trading, insiders would prefer to exploit their superior information about their own firm by trading in their own stock rather than using it indirectly by trading in the peer stocks. However,Mehta et al. (2021)argue that insiders trade profitably in related stocks in response to greater media coverage of insider trading and the associated increase in perceived legal risk. Thus, in our setting, we conjecture that when stricter laws limit insider trading in their own stock, insiders will apply their information to the next best use, that is, trading in peer stocks. Additionally, after the regulation, they would be less concerned about trading in peer stocks against better-informed traded-stock insiders whose trades would be curtailed by the law. Whereas insiders may trade in peer stocks for various reasons even before the change in regulation, the new restrictions create an additional motive to trade in them, that is, to compensate for the lost trading opportunities in their own stock. Thus, we hypothesize that, after the new regulation, insiders trade less in their own stocks and more in peer stocks. We define peer stocks as stocks in the same industry as the insider’s own firm becauseBen-David et al. (2019)find that insiders trade profitably in other stocks in the same industry.2 As a baseline, we first establish that the likelihood of own-stock trades in a given quarter and the associated abnormal returns from such trades decrease after controlling for individual insider’s skill through insider fixed effects.3These results show that the insider trading regulation is effective in curbing informed trading by insiders in their own stocks. This is where most papers examining the effectiveness of insider trading laws stop. In contrast, we provide evidence that insiders change their trading strategy and use information that was previously used for own-stock trades for peer-stock trades. In particular, informed trading in peer stocks increases when (i) insiders’ information is more fungible toward peer stocks, and (ii) there are greater restrictions on trading in own stocks. We now describe these results in detail. We capture the effect of fungible information on increased peer stock trading in multiple ways. First, we find that there is an increase in the likelihood of peer-stock trades only when the traded stock has high comovement with the industry, which enables better use of industry information possessed by the insiders. Second, there is an improvement in abnormal profits of peer-stock trades after the new regulation. It is driven by the average industry component and not the traded firm-specific component. Third, we measure the fungibility of information using pairwise correlation over the preregulation period between an own stock and each peer stock and document a bigger increase in peer trades’ abnormal returns when this correlation is high. Finally, if a peer-stock trade substitutes a forgone hypothetical trade in the insider’s own stock, we would expect that the forgone own-firm trade is profitable, and this profitability is the primary source of the profitability of the implemented peer trade. We find support for this conjecture, suggesting that the increased profitability is driven by information about thetrader’s own firmand not by firm-specific information about thetraded stock. Overall, this lack of evidence on traded-firm-specific returns suggests that there is no tipping by the traded stock’s insiders. The “no-tipping” evidence reflects the strict restrictions in the regulation (old and new) on the communication of UPSI by an insider. We also find that peer-stock trading is more profitable when there are greater restrictions on own-stock trading: (i) during a window around earnings announcements of own stock when there is a blackout on trading by insiders, and (ii) when the own firm has better governance given its role in restricting insider trading.4Thus, the greater the risks of trading in own stock, the more the incentives to shift to peer-stock trading. Taken together, our results reflect the balancing act that insiders undertake to minimize legal liability while exploiting their information advantage. Our findings of reduced informed trading in own stock and increased cross-trading in peer stocks, triggered by insider trading laws, imply an alteration in the mix of informed traders in any given firm—fewer own-stock insiders but more peer-stock traders. A natural question that follows is how this change affects information flow to capital markets, information asymmetry, and stock liquidity, and we build on the longstanding academic debate on the pros and cons of laws that restrict informed insider trading. For our next set of analyses, using a sample of all BSE-listed stocks, we examine the effect of insider trading laws and the consequent cross-trading by peer-firm insiders on illiquidity and stock price informativeness, two attributes the regulators seek to improve. Restriction on insider trading is expected to reduce adverse selection concerns of uninformed investors, improving liquidity (Leland 1992). However, liquidity improvements may be dampened because of increased informed trades by peer-firm managers. As a result, the adverse selection problem could persist even after the insider trading regulation. Using theAmihud (2002)measure of illiquidity for the firms of identified insiders, we predict and find that liquidity improves for firms with low expected peer-insider trading, proxied by low industry comovement of the stock. But this effect is significantly dampened for stocks with high industry comovement. Thus, the emergence of this new class of informed traders (peer-firm insiders), who could potentially engage in informed trades, hurts market liquidity. The net effect is that liquidity does not improve for high-comovement firms after the regulation. Insiders’ trades in their own stocks and the disclosure of such trades can convey private information that may be otherwise difficult to disclose credibly to the market.5Thus, when insiders limit informed trading in their own stocks, there can be a reduction in the price informativeness (Manne 1966,Carlton and Fischel 1983,Udpa 1996,Choi et al. 2023). On the other hand, the incentives of outsiders for information acquisition may improve with stricter insider trading regulation, thus improving price informativeness (Fishman and Hagerty 1992,Bushman et al. 2005).Fernandes and Ferreira (2009)find that following the first-time implementation of insider trading regulation, prices become more informative in developed, but not emerging, markets. Both the mechanisms through which insider trading in own stocks affects price informativeness are likely to be weakened by peer trading. We followKacperczyk et al. (2021)to measure price informativeness by how well stock prices predict future earnings. We also use two other alternative measures used by them: jump ratio and adjusted variance ratio. We find evidence that prices have become more informative after the regulation for low-comovement firms. This suggests that insider laws prevent the crowding out of information by insiders, and there is an improvement in price informativeness in the absence of peer trading. But this improvement is fully or partly offset for high-comovement firms. Further, the ability of prices to predict firm-specific future earnings improves, but the ability to forecast industry earnings deteriorates after the regulation. Thus, the price-informativeness benefits of stricter insider trading restrictions are realized only when the likelihood of peer-insider trades in a given stock is low. We contribute to several strands of literature on insider trading. First, we further the literature on the effectiveness of insider trading regulation. SeeJaffe (1974b),Seyhun (1992),Bhattacharya and Daouk (2002),Fernandes and Ferreira (2009), andWhite (2020), among others. We contribute to this literature by highlighting a new mechanism, peer trading, by which insider trading laws affect liquidity and price informativeness. We document that in the presence of peer trading, stricter insider trading laws, which have to balance the burden of compliance with integrity and fairness in financial markets, may be limited in producing the desired benefits of improved liquidity and better price informativeness. A small but growing literature assesses how insiders change their trading behavior in their own stock response to changing regulation, enforcement, or legal risk. Insiders trading in their own stocks become less aggressive with greater SEC resources for enforcement (Del Guercio et al. 2017) and higher legal risk (Kacperczyk and Pagnotta 2024) but more profitable with lower risk of lawsuits (Adhikari et al. 2021). We add to this literature by showing that, in response to insider trading restrictions, insiders engage in informed trading in peer firms instead of trading in their own stock. Lastly, our paper extends the literature examining insiders trading in stocks other than their own (for example, see theoretical models, such as inChen and Jorgensen 2021). A few recent studies innovatively capture insider trades in other stocks to overcome the limitation that such data are not readily available (Tookes 2008,Johannesson 2018,Ben-David et al. 2019,Berkman et al. 2020).6Ben-David et al. (2019)document that insiders trade profitably in other industry stocks based on industry familiarity.Mehta et al. (2021)document increased informed trading in economically linked firms (“shadow trading”) around news events of a source firm, particularly following greater news media coverage of traditional “illegal” insider trading. We reaffirm the results in these two studies in a new context by showing that peer trading intensifies in the face of stricter insider trading regulation. Our study advances this literature in multiple ways. First, we document the implications of such peer trading on market liquidity and price informativeness, two important market quality variables that regulators care about. Thus, our paper links this literature to the one about the effectiveness of insider trading regulation discussed in the previous paragraph. Second, our paper, which uses a change in law, complements the analysis byMehta et al. (2021), who use news media coverage to proxy for greater risk to traditional insider trading. Whereas the ability of external monitors, such as media and institutional investors, is constrained byexistinglaws, the regulators have the ability tochangethe laws. In the process, they must make trade-offs, and our study informs this regulatory choice. Finally, based on analysis using the identity of the trader and the timing of the trade, we can conclude that the regulatory change increases peer trading based on information common between the trader’s own stock and the traded stock, rather than tipping by traded stock’s insiders. Whereas the study uses Indian data, it has wider relevance, as we discuss in detail next.
article.2. Institutional Background: 2.1. The Indian Setting As argued in the introduction, the Indian context enables us to study the trade-offs involved in a new insider trading regulation and its consequences. Given the more settled laws on insider trading, the developed markets are unlikely to have undergone a change in the law in recent times. In addition, a study on laws and financial markets in India does have broader relevance for multiple reasons. First, English is widely spoken in India. The country shares English legal origin (La Porta et al. 1998) and consequent similarity of legal institutions with many other countries. Second, following the liberalization of the economy in 1991, the reforms in Indian financial markets have made them similar to the developed markets in many ways. Finally, Indian accounting and financial data are generally of good quality, and recent studies published in top-tier journals have used them to answer important questions.7These additional advantages make a compelling case for the use of Indian data for this study. 2.2. Insider Trading Regulation in India The Securities and Exchange Board of India is the securities regulator in India and has the power to make regulations consistent with the objectives of the Securities and Exchange Act of 1992. The SEBI first addressed insider trading regulation in the Prohibition of Insider Trading Regulations of 1992 (PITR 1992). After a series of minor amendments between 2002 and 2011, it decided to overhaul and update the regulation. This decision followed the updated corporate law, the Companies Act of 2013, which aimed to improve corporate governance in India. The Sodhi Committee was set up in 2013 to review the PITR 1992. It made a number of recommendations “focused on making this area of regulation more predictable, precise and clear by suggesting a combination of principles-based regulations.” SEBI adopted the new regulation, the Prohibition of Insider Trading Regulations, 2015 (PITR 2015), on January 15, 2015.8 Some of the key changes made by the 2015 regulation were as follows. (i) There was clarification and expansion of the scope of an insider, defined as a connected person9or anyone who could have access to UPSI regardless of the source of information. (ii) The onus of proof was put on the connected persons (insiders) that they were not in possession of unpublished price-sensitive information at the time of the trade. In all other cases (including peer trades), the onus was on the SEBI. This is a crucial difference between the Indian regulation compared with the United States.10(iii) The option of trading plans for insiders who may have continuous access to UPSI was introduced. (iv) A more serious role was given to the compliance officer at each company to police, monitor, and regulate trading by employees and connected persons. This puts a greater burden on the firm and board to ensure compliance with the regulation. (v) The provision was added that SEBI could take action against any violation of the regulations, simplifying a previously lengthy and ineffective process. Overall, the PITR 2015 was designed to address widespread insider trading by requiring greater compliance and monitoring while increasing the burden of proof on the alleged insider. Thus, the insider trading law was significantly strengthened in 2015, and the onus of enforcement partially shifted to the company. As noted in the introduction, the financial news media did perceive the new regulation to be stricter and more in line with global standards. Still, one potential concern is the lack of enforcement of insider trading laws in developing economies, andBhattacharya and Daouk (2009)argue that no insider trading law is better than unenforced laws. At the time the new regulation was passed, there was an expectation in the media that the enforcement would improve.11Damle and Zaveri (2022)provide evidence that, in general, the enforcement by the SEBI indeed improved in the period between 2014 and 2018. Further, the International Monetary Fund’s Financial Sector Assessment Programme of India 2017 noted significant progress toward enforcement by the SEBI from the time of the previous assessment in 2011.12These facts, taken together with the provision regarding putting the burden of proof on insiders (point (ii) in the previous paragraph), provide the underlying mechanism for the effectiveness of the new regulation in curbing insiders’ trading in their own stocks. Further, we can gauge the effectiveness of the law by the change in the insiders’ behavior. As we document in our tests inSection 4, the insiders’ trades in their own stocks did become less frequent and less informed after the new regulation. This provides additional validity that the change in the law did have teeth. 2.3. Stock Exchanges India has two major stock exchanges, the BSE, established in 1875, and the National Stock Exchange (NSE), established in 1992. The BSE is the largest securities market in India, with over 5,000 companies listed on it, and the NSE has almost 2,000 stocks listed on it. Whereas the BSE has more stocks listed, the volume of shares traded tends to be higher on the NSE.13Both the BSE and the NSE are in the top 10 exchanges globally by market capitalization.14Insiders are required to disclose insider trades to the stock exchange(s). This is the primary source of the insider trading data we use in our study.
article.3. Data: Our broad approach to constructing the sample involves matching the reported insider trades to a detailed transaction-level data set from the BSE. We describe it in detail in this section. 3.1. Insider Trading Data Insider trading regulation mandates that directors, officers, and substantial shareholders in a listed Indian company report trades that exceed INR one million (about $12,114) over a calendar quarter to the stock exchange(s) where the firm is listed within two trading days.15Thus, one limitation of our analysis is that the trades that fall below this threshold may not be reported and, hence, cannot be used for matching an insider to a trader. We obtain these filings from the Prowess database by the Centre for Monitoring the Indian Economy (CMIE). The disclosure includes the transaction date, insider’s name, stock identifier, mode of acquisition or sale, and the number of shares transacted. The filings can pertain to transactions other than a trade on an exchange, such as allotment of shares under employee stock ownership plans, off-market transactions, and the exercise of employee stock options. We only retain the insider-reported trades executed on the secondary market. 3.2. BSE Trade Data We use a proprietary data set that covers all the trades that have been executed on the BSE over a period of six years, from 2012 to 2017. The details include the date of the trade, stock identifier, number of shares, trade price, broker identifiers (IDs), and trader (broker’s client) IDs for both sides of the transaction (buy and sell). Every broker assigns a unique ID to each client; thus, we can uniquely identify a trader for all trades executed through a given broker based on the broker ID–client ID pair. The client and the broker IDs are anonymized but stay constant for a given broker and client for the entire period. Thus, to the extent an investor trades through the same broker, we can track that investor’s trading activity on the BSE for our sample period. This feature is particularly valuable, as it allows us to match insider trades more accurately to a trader, which is not possible using typical publicly available transaction databases that contain no identifiers for the trading parties. For example,Inci et al. (2010)use the TAQ data to match the reported insider trades to executed transactions. It is important to caveat that (i) our data does not include trades by a given insider that were executed on the NSE because we only have access to the BSE trades, and (ii) we do not include trades executed on the BSE by a given insider with another broker if we did not match that broker-client pair to the insider at the matching step. We do not expect these exclusions to bias our results because insiders have no reason to systematically change their trading behavior between exchanges and brokers in different ways for their own stocks and peer stocks around the regulation. Therefore, whereas we do not look at a comprehensive set of insider trades, we study a comparable subset of trades by insiders in the pre- and postregulation periods. This is similar to the setting inBen-David et al. (2019), who examine insiders’ trades executed through one large discount broker. 3.3. Sample Period We utilize PITR 2015 to cleanly identify a shock to insider trading regulation. Though the regulation was adopted on January 15, 2015, the Companies Act of 2013 enumerates the provision for insider trading restriction (without providing explicit guidelines for it). Also, following the Companies Act of 2013, the draft rules were published, and public comments were solicited before the law was finally adopted on January 15, 2015. Hence, we view the period between September 12, 2013 (the effective date for the Companies Act) and January 15, 2015 (the adoption of PITR 2015) as a transition period with significant regulatory uncertainty, and we eliminate it from the analysis. In summary, the preregulation period spans from January 1, 2012, through September 12, 2013, and the postregulation period spans from January 15, 2015, to December 31, 2017. Our results are robust to alternative transition periods, as discussed inSection 4.5and Section A.4.2 in the Online Appendix. 3.4. Sample Construction We begin with the reported insider trades during our sample period to detect potential insiders in the BSE trade data set. Then, for every reported insider trade, we identify all the traders in the BSE data that have traded in the insiders’ own firm on the given date. We separately aggregate the number of shares bought and sold on that date for each trader. We aggregate the shares at a daily level for a trader to account for the possibility that a trade may be split during execution, but the insiders report the total number of shares traded. The idea is to match an insider (who reports trades) uniquely to a trader account in the BSE trade data and be reasonably confident that the account belongs to the insider. The process involves uniquely matching a reported trade to a BSE trade based on the transaction date, stock identifier, number of shares transacted, and direction of the trade (acquisition or sale). Such a matching process often results in a single reported insider trade being matched to multiple trades in the BSE trade data set. This usually occurs when the insider’s trade is a round-lot trade and a popular trade size for many traders on that date.16Therefore, to enable a unique match between an insider and a BSE trader, we focus on insiders with at least two reported trades, with at least one being an odd-lot trade during the sample period. The matching process is executed in two stages. In the first stage, we only consider the odd-lot trades and try to match them to a trader account in the BSE trade data. At this stage, one of the three possible outcomes may occur for the reported insider trade: (i) the trade matches uniquely to an account, (ii) the trade matches multiple accounts, or (iii) the trade does not match any trading account.17For outcomes (ii) or (iii), we drop the insider trade from subsequent analysis. In case of a unique match, that is, outcome (i), we consider the trader account in the BSE data as a potential insider account, and this insider-trader account pair is analyzed further in the next stage.18 In the second stage, we require that an insider with a “potential insider account” matches uniquely to the same account for at least one more reported trade. In case of a unique match again, we attribute the trader account in the BSE data to the corresponding insider. Overall, the two-stage process ensures that an insider matches uniquely to an account at least twice, and at least one of those unique matches is for an odd-lot trade. This gives us further confidence in the insider-BSE trader account match. Overall, we match 11,090 reported trades and 1,312 reporting insiders to an account in the BSE data. Once a trader account in the BSE data is mapped to an insider, we extract all trades executed through this account, enabling us to identify trades by insiders in stocks other than their own. Next, we obtain the financial data and stock returns for the own stocks and peer stocks from CMIE’s Prowess database. We define peer stocks as stocks in the same industry as the insider’s own firm. We evaluate the profitability of the trades using six-month buy-and-hold returns for each own and peer-stock-trade through insider accounts. We drop the trades with missing returns from the sample. Lastly, we exclude the insiders in the finance industry from the sample. Finance industry insiders are likely to possess broad interindustry information because of lending relationships. Unlike insiders in other industries, they may use their superior private information to trade more broadly, that is, in nonpeer firms. After these steps, we have 943 insiders with 17,367 total trades: 13,912 (80%) in their own stocks,19with an average size of INR 3.5 million, and 3,455 (20%) in peer stocks, with an average size of INR 0.5 million.20In a similar process of matching trades at a large discount brokerage to reported insider trades in the United States,Ben-David et al. (2019)identify 105 brokerage accounts as belonging to insiders of 171 publicly traded firms. In their sample, 785 (65%) are own-stock trades, and 416 (35%) are other stocks in the same industry. Thus, all insiders have, on average, 7.5 trades in their own stocks and 4 trades in peer stocks, the corresponding numbers in our sample being 14.7 and 3.6, respectively.
article.4. Probability and Profitability of Insi: We conduct two broad categories of tests to examine the change in behavior by corporate insiders following the change in regulation: (i) evaluation of the likelihood of an insider trade in own and peer stock, and (ii) examination of the profitability of insiders’ own and peer-stock trades.21Further, we investigate the role of access to fungible information in changing the trading behavior of insiders. We describe these tests and results in this section. 4.1. Likelihood of Own- and Peer-Stock Trades Are insiders more or less likely to trade in their own firm’s stock and peer stocks after the new regulation? We examine this using insider-insiders’ own-firm announcement quarters as our unit of observation, which results in a sample of 19,459. We measure a firm-announcement quarter for each own firm from the previous earnings announcement date + 2 days to the current quarter earnings announcement date − 1 day. An insider may have multiple own firms, for each of which the announcement quarter would be different. Hence, the unit of analysis is insider-insiders’ own-firm-announcement quarters rather than just insider quarter.22We examine the likelihood of a trade in own and peer stocks by estimating the following linear probability model. Panel A ofTable 1presents descriptive statistics of the variables in this analysis. We see that the average probability of an own-stock trade in insider-insiders’ own-firm announcement quarter is 0.15, and that of a peer-stock trade is 0.05. We construct a measure of the comovement of a peer stock’s returns with the returns of the remaining firms in the industry by adapting thePiotroski and Roulstone (2004)synchronicity measure. We regress the firm’s weekly stock returns on the current and prior week’s value-weighted industry returns (excluding the firm’s returns). The firm-specific measure of comovement is defined as the log of the ratio ofR2and (1−R2) obtained from the regression. Thus, we expect that the probability of trade by an insider is more likely to go up in a high-comovement peer stock than in a low-comovement one. These results indicate that insiders are more likely to trade in a peer stock where the usability of their information about the industry is high. 4.2. Profitability of Own- and Peer-Stock Trades To evaluate the profitability of trades executed by insiders in their own stock and peer stocks, we calculate abnormal returns for each trade using the approach inDaniel et al. (1997)(with the modifications specified inWermers 2003). Specifically, we construct 125 portfolios by sequentially sorting the universe of the stocks based on quintiles of log market capitalization, book-to-market ratio, and momentum. Next, the overall DGTW-adjusted return for a stock is calculated as the stock’s six-month buy-and-hold return less the six-month buy-and-hold return for the value-weighted DGTW portfolio to which the stock belongs.24We winsorize the returns at the 1st and 99th percentiles. Panel B ofTable 1shows the overall descriptive statistics for returns on own-stock and peer-stock trades. We see that relative to the average return of around 0.03, the standard deviation is substantial at 0.38. We first examine the univariate returns for the insider trades in own stock and peer stocks inTable 3. Source of Changes in Profitability In this subsection, we test the hypothesis that insiders use fungible information to trade profitably in peer stocks by examining the source of profitability of these trades. 4.3.1. Trades Using Common Industry Information. We first focus on a coarse measure of information fungibility: common industry information. Specifically, we examine the changes in profitability around the regulation coming from industry- and firm-specific components of returns. If the increase in peer stock trades after the regulation is driven by fungible information, such as common industry information, we expect the rise in profitability of peer trades to be because of the industry component of returns. To the extent that insiders are experts in the industry and can assess the differential impact of industry news on individual firms within the industry, we expect the profitability of such trades to be driven by firm-specific returns. Such trades would have been attractive before the regulation, and the law should not have affected these trades. Industry Ret, the industry component of abnormal returns for a given insider trade, is computed as the value-weighted DGTW-adjusted returns for all stocks in a given industry for the six-month period starting on the date of a given insider’s trade.Firm-specific Ret, therefore, is the firm’s overall DGTW-adjusted return lessIndustry Ret. We run the specification inEquation (2)with these separate components as dependent variables with either industry or insider fixed effects. The results are reported inTable 4. Columns (2) and (5) report the results forIndustry Ret, and Columns (3) and (6) forFirm-specific Ret. For peer-stock trades, there is a significant increase in the industry component of the returns, but not firm-specific returns, as seen from the coefficient forPost. These results are consistent with insiders using common industry information to trade in peer stocks after the regulation. As an alternative strategy, insiders could exchange nonpublic own firm–specific information with peer-firm managers, who could then use it to trade profitably in peer stocks. Such cross-tipping-based trading strategies would result in higher peer-firm-specific returns. We find no support for change in this alternative information channel, as evident from the insignificant coefficient forPostin column (3) and column (6). There is a significant reduction in the firm-specific component in own-stock returns across both specifications.β3, as well asβ2+β3, are negative and significant at the 1% level, indicating that insiders reduce the use of firm-specific information while trading in their own stocks. This is consistent with the objectives of the insider trading law. Interestingly, however, the industry component of returns of own trades increases after the regulation, with bothβ3andβ2+β3positive and significant. These results suggest that after the new law, insiders alter the type of information they use for their trades, relying more on the industry component of their information and scaling back on using firm-specific news even while trading in their own stocks. Overall, the results inTables 2and4suggest that information fungibility owing to common industry information contributes to insiders trading profitably in peer stocks after the regulation. 4.3.2. In the absence of restriction, they could have profitably traded in their own stock. It is important to highlight one assumption underlying the analysis in this subsection. While calculating the pseudo-own-stock returns, we have assumed that the best hypothetical trade in the own stock, without stricter regulation, would have been identical to the trade in the peer stock. But if the insiders were to take advantage of their information by trading their own stock, they might have structured their trade differently to make the best use of their information in that situation. Thus, the pseudoreturn we calculate provides a lower bound on the profitability of the trade foregone in own stock. Based on this lower bound, our evidence suggests that the foregone trades in own stocks would have been no less and potentially more profitable than the actual trades in peer stocks. Thus, the evidence supports the argument that when faced with stricter insider trading law, insiders switch from trading in their own stock to trading in peer stocksusing the information about their own stock. 4.4. Differential Restrictions on Trading in Own Stock So far, we have established that in response to stricter regulation preventing trading in their own stock, insiders shift their informed trading to peer stocks. We would expect this effect to be stronger when there are greater consequences of trading in own stock after the new regulation. In this subsection, we test this hypothesis using earnings announcements and governance at an insider’s own firm. 4.4.1. Trading Around Earnings Announcements. Similar to the older regulation, PITR 2015 mandates that firms prevent their insiders from trading when in possession of UPSI via the closure of the permissible trading window. In compliance with this mandate, firms typically do not allow insiders to trade in their own stocks around earnings announcements (blackout period).27Hence, the stringency of the new regulation would be particularly salient around an insider’s own firm’s earnings announcement, and insiders would face greater consequences for trading in their own stock around earnings announcements than for trading in peer stocks. To examine this prediction, we define an EA window:t− 10 days tot+ 1 days around the earnings announcement t of the insider’s own company. We find that, after the regulation, there is an improvement in profitability of the insider trades in peer stocks in the EA window as well as outside it. However, consistent with the prediction, the magnitude of the improvement is larger for trades during the EA window. Section A.3.1 and Table A.1 in the Online Appendix present these results in detail. 4.4.2. Governance. Stronger corporate governance, because of better board monitoring, is known to restrict insider trading (Ravina and Sapienza 2010,Jagolinzer et al. 2011,Cziraki et al. 2014,Dai et al. 2016). To the extent insiders at firms with stronger governance face greater negative consequences of trading in their own stocks, we expect these insiders in better-governed firms to have stronger incentives to take their informed trading to peer stocks. We examine this possibility as described in more detail in Section A.3.2, with results in Table A.2 in the Online Appendix. The new regulation is more effective for high-governance firms, as seen from significantly lowerFirm-specific Retfrom own stock trading in the postperiod. However, after the new regulation, insiders in these high-governance firms also trade more profitably in peer stocks using the industry component, presumably to compensate for reduced opportunities in own stocks. Results inSections 4.4.1and4.4.2support the hypothesis that insiders shift their informed trading to peer stocks more strongly when trading in their own stock has greater consequences. This further bolsters our main conclusion that when faced with a stricter insider trading regime, insiders substitute own-stock trades with informed trades in peer stocks. 4.5. (SeeLakonishok and Lee 2001,Ke et al. 2003,Piotroski and Roulstone 2005,Kallunki et al. 2009, andVeenman 2012for asymmetric informativeness of insider buys versus sells.) The results so far demonstrate that owing to stricter insider trading regulations, insiders’ trading in their own stock decreases, and informed trading in peer stock increases. In the next section, we examine the consequences of this shift.

Changes in Stock Price Informativeness Next, we examine the effect of insider trading regulation on the price informativeness of stocks. Even without considering peer trading, the implications for insider trading regulation on price informativeness are ambiguous. Because insiders trade on value-relevant information about their own firms, they help make prices more informative, as this information gets impounded in prices. Thus, curbing insider trading is likely to result in less informative prices (Manne 1966,Carlton and Fischel 1983,Udpa 1996,Choi et al. 2023). On the other hand, insider trading can discourage information acquisition by outsiders (Fishman and Haggerty 1992,Bushman et al. 2005). In such a case, restricting insider trading will encourage more information production, thus improving price informativeness. The overall effect on price informativeness depends on which of the two channels dominates. Indeed,Fernandes and Ferreira (2009)find that insider trading laws improve price informativeness in developed markets but not in emerging markets. Our results so far mean that in response to the new regulation, insiders reduce informed trading in their own stock but increase it in peer stocks. For both channels described earlier, we expect the effect on price informativeness of increased peer trading to offset the effect of reduced trading by own insiders. This countervailing impact is likely to be weaker for two reasons: (i) the information of peer-firm insiders is likely to be noisier than that of own-firm insiders, and (ii) whereas insiders are required to report trades in their own stock, peer-firm insiders are not required to report their trades. On the other hand, more informed traders (peer insiders) competing against each other will incorporate the information in prices faster, improving the price informativeness (Holden and Subrahmanyam 1992). Via this channel, the offsetting effect of increased peer trading can be stronger than that of reduced own-insider trading. Thus, what the net result is in any given situation is an empirical question. FollowingKacperczyk et al. Overall, the results inTables 8and9, along with the earlier profitability results, point toward insiders crowding out information acquisition by outsiders. To the extent insiders’ informed trading is curbed (low comovement firms, trading based on firm-specific information), prices become more informative. When insiders increase their informed trading based on common information (high-comovement firms, trading based on industry information), price informativeness does not improve or even deteriorates. Taken together, the results inTables 7–9suggest that when insiders divert their superior information to trade profitably in peer stocks, both the benefits of improved liquidity and greater price informativeness are attenuated. Overall, the increase in peer-firm trading appears to dampen the intended benefits of the insider trading regulation.
article.6. Conclusion: Insiders face a trade-off between trading on the more precise information they have about their own stock and the potential legal noncompliance risks. Changes in regulation that increase noncompliance costs, particularly for own-stock trades, alter the cost-benefit trade-off. We construct a novel data set of trades on the BSE by insiders in their own and peer stocks and document that, when faced with stricter insider trading regulations in India, insiders trade more often and more profitably in peer-firm stocks, especially when insiders are more likely to have fungible information. Increased cross-trading by peer-firm managers offsets the benefits—increased liquidity and improved price efficiency—of reduced trading by insiders in their own firms. These effects have been largely ignored in the prior literature, but they have implications for insider trading regulations. Taken together, our findings highlight the trade-offs for regulators who need to balance market integrity with the burden of compliance.

AItitle: 内幕监管收紧催生跨市套利：行业信息可替代性削弱流动性改善
AIsummary: 这篇由Agarwal等人撰写的论文聚焦金融市场监管领域，探讨了内幕交易法规严格化对市场行为的意外后果。研究核心问题是：当法律限制内部人交易自家股票时，他们是否会转向交易同行股票以规避监管，从而削弱政策效果？此前研究多关注法规对自家股票交易的直接影响，但作者首次系统性揭示了“同行股票替代效应”的存在。为验证假设，作者创新性地构建了印度孟买证券交易所（BSE）2012-2017年的独特数据集，通过匿名交易者ID将内部人申报交易与实际交易记录精准匹配，追踪到943名内部人超过17,000笔交易。利用印度2015年《内幕交易禁止条例》作为自然实验，研究发现：法规实施后内部人交易自家股票概率下降22%，而同行股票交易概率上升20%，且后者盈利性显著提高。这种转移效应在行业关联性强、信息可替代性高的股票中尤为明显。通过流动性指标（Amihud测度）和价格信息含量分析，证实同行交易削弱了法规预期的市场流动性改善和价格发现功能。该研究突破性地揭示了监管套利行为对政策效果的抵消作用，为平衡市场公平与信息效率的监管设计提供了重要实证依据。
Highlights:
学术要点 1：信息可替代性（Fungible Information） 指内幕信息在行业同行股票间的跨公司适用性，使内幕人员可利用行业层面的私有信息进行跨股票套利。|||
学术要点 2：同步性度量（Comovement Measure） 通过股票收益率与行业收益率的同步性指标，量化信息可替代性的实施路径，反映行业信息在跨公司交易中的可迁移程度。|||
学术要点 3：匿名交易标识符（Anonymized Trader IDs） 通过经纪商-客户ID对追踪同一交易者跨股票交易行为的技术方法，突破传统内幕交易数据仅披露本公司交易的局限。|||
学术要点 4：交易计划（Trading Plans） 印度新规允许持续接触未公开信息的内幕人员预先申报交易策略的制度设计，形成监管漏洞下的替代性交易通道。|||
学术要点 5：法律风险转移（Burden of Proof Shift） 印度2015年新规将举证责任倒置于内幕人员（仅限本公司交易），形成"本公司交易-同行交易"的差异化监管套利空间。
